---
title: "Choosing response families"
output: rmarkdown::html_vignette
vignette: >
  %\VignetteIndexEntry{Choosing response families}
  %\VignetteEngine{knitr::rmarkdown}
  %\VignetteEncoding{UTF-8}
---

```{r, include = FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(collapse = TRUE, comment = "#>")
```

This article will help users choose response families for distributional
regression data: continuous outcomes, counts, proportions, percentages,
positive measurements, shape-sensitive outcomes, and ordinal scores. Examples
emphasize ecological, evolutionary, and environmental applications.

The current planning reference is `docs/design/06-distribution-roadmap.md`.

Meta-analysis is not listed as a separate family. It uses `family = gaussian()`
with `meta_known_V(V = V)`.

## Implemented continuous families

Gaussian location-scale models use `mu` and `sigma`:

```r
drmTMB(
  bf(y ~ temperature, sigma ~ treatment),
  family = gaussian(),
  data = dat
)
```

Student-t location-scale-shape models add `nu`, the degrees-of-freedom or tail
weight parameter:

```text
y_i | mu_i, sigma_i, nu_i ~ Student-t(mu_i, sigma_i, nu_i)
nu_i = 2 + exp(eta_nu_i)
```

```r
drmTMB(
  bf(y ~ temperature, sigma ~ treatment, nu ~ 1),
  family = student(),
  data = dat
)
```

Use this first Student-t path when a continuous response is mostly well
described by a location-scale model but has heavier-tailed residuals than a
Gaussian model. Random effects, meta-analytic known covariance, phylogenetic
terms, and bivariate Student-t models are planned later. The tutorial
"Robust continuous responses" shows the equation, syntax, diagnostics, and a
Gaussian comparison for this family.

For bivariate models, the preferred public direction is to combine response
families:

```r
family = c(gaussian(), gaussian())
family = list(gaussian(), gaussian())
family = c(gaussian(), poisson())
```

This keeps mixed-response bivariate models natural. In ecological examples,
this might mean body mass with fecundity counts, survival with dispersal
counts, or leaf area with seed number. The all-Gaussian composed case is
implemented for both `c()` and `list()` spellings and currently routes to the
same likelihood as `biv_gaussian()`. Mixed-response composed families remain
future work.
